mnemonics.title: SPOOFIR Data-Sharing Attributes
mnemonics.content:
S - Shared (default for globals/heap)
P - Private (uninitialized copies)
O - Firstprivate (initialized copies)
O - Lastprivate (copy back last iteration)
F - False sharing (cache line contention)
I - Inherits (from enclosing region)
R - Reduction (associative ops with identity)

mnemonics.title: Race Condition Detection
mnemonics.content: When MULTIPLE threads ACCESS shared memory, with at least one WRITE, and NO synchronization EXISTS - that's a RACE!

mnemonics.title: Default Data Sharing Rule
mnemonics.content: GHOST: Global and Static = SHARED; Heap = SHARED; Automatic variables inside parallel region = SHARED; Only variables declared WITHIN the parallel region are PRIVATE

acronyms.expansion: Single Program Multiple Data - pattern where all threads execute the same code but on different data
acronyms.usage: Used when parallel regions contain different data subsets but identical logic

acronyms.expansion: Single Instruction Multiple Data - pattern where the same operation is applied to multiple data elements simultaneously
acronyms.usage: Used in vectorization and GPU computing; relevant to understanding parallel execution models

acronyms.expansion: Thread-Level Parallelism - parallelism achieved by using multiple concurrent threads
acronyms.usage: OpenMP primarily targets TLP, as opposed to data-level parallelism (DLP)

acronyms.expansion: Performance degradation when threads modify variables sharing the same cache line
acronyms.usage: Mitigated by padding variables or using private copies

visualTips.visualization: Draw a memory diagram showing: Main Memory (shared variables), with each thread having a separate Private Stack area. Shared variables point to single memory location; Private variables show separate boxes for each thread with 'X' indicating uninitialized or copies.

Draw a Gantt chart with two threads (T1, T2) on Y-axis and time on X-axis. Show: T1 reads x=5, T2 reads x=5, T1 writes x=6, T2 writes x=6 (LOST UPDATE) - result should be 7 but is 6. This visual demonstrates why concurrent writes lose data.

Draw: Original 'sum' variable in main memory. Arrow to 4 thread boxes, each showing 'sum_private = 0' (initialization). After loop: partial sums (e.g., 100, 250, 325, 175) in each thread. Final arrow combining all with '+' operation to get 850.

analogies.analogy: Think of a shared whiteboard in an office (anyone can read/write, needs coordination) vs. a personal notepad (each person has their own, no coordination needed). Without proper management of the whiteboard, different people writing simultaneously creates chaos - that's a race condition.
Like giving each team member a COPY of the project brief before they start working independently. The original stays unchanged, but each person has the starting information they need.
Like the 'final report' from the last person to speak in a meeting becoming the official team report. The individual contributions (private) are combined into the final output (original variable).
Like collecting donations from each person (threads) in a room and then one person counts the total. Each person's count is separate until combined - that's reduction!
